paragraphs.body: AI and the Composition of Music: Why AI Should Not Be Dependent on to Compose Music  University of Pittsburgh

AI and the Composition of Music: Why AI Should Not Be Dependent on to Compose Music Music has been recorded throughout history and in multiple different cultures. Even though these cultures had no way of interacting with or connecting with each other, music was present in almost every single one of them. Even when the cultures discovered each other but were unable to communicate due to a language difference, music was always enjoyed by both sides. According to Montagu, “from the Aurignacian period, which starts around 43,000 BP in eastern Europe and around 40,000 in the west, to the Magdalenian and later, ending around 10,000 BP, which we have a very considerable number of instruments, plus a few representations” (2017). Montagu explains that instruments and representations of those instruments have been found, which date back to 43,000 years before the year 1950. So, music is old, and with the advancement of the world, so did music advance and improve. Today, with the advancement of AI we face a new rising problem of AI. AI (artificial intelligence) has advanced to a point where it can solve papers, generate images, and scour the internet for a specific type of information in seconds. Although all these advances are good, there is one that isn’t, and that is AI’s ability to compose music. People may not see it as a problem, but the use of AI in the composition of music is a problem because AI music doesn't affect listeners' emotions the same way human music does. Additionally, AI-generated music will raise challenges regarding ownership and copyright laws. A solution to these two problems is using musicians to create their own new and original music and then using AI to improve/perfect it. AI and its ability to comprehend human emotions The first point I’m going to talk about is AI’s ability to comprehend emotion compared to humans, and how humans’ way of using these emotions is better than AI’s. AI may be able to understand the concept of emotions. Fowler supports this claim by stating that “AI doesn’t possess the biological systems that generate emotions in humans. While it can mimic emotional responses, it doesn’t “feel” them” (2023). We know that most music is based on emotion, whether it’s happiness, sadness, or excitement, they can all be represented in music. This usually requires the music artist to actually feel the emotions and then express/turn them into the music that he is creating. While AI may be able to mimic these emotions, it will not be genuine, and this will affect how people will perceive the music, especially when it is easy to tell the difference between AI and humans. Especially when some people pay for their music either by premium subscriptions or by purchasing physical copies, people are going to be expecting something genuine and real. Artificial music produced by AI will also have a negative perception by professionals who can tell when something isn’t real. A study conducted by Moura states that “results from the online survey indicated a rather negative perception, low purchase intention for AI music and a negative credibility perception of musicians using AI. Findings from the experiment indicated no significant differences between the groups, suggesting that the awareness of the use of automation did not influence the perception towards the music.” (2021).

annotations.comment: For direct quotes, please include page numbers in the citation.

paragraphs.body: The study even states that since there were no significant differences between the groups, people didn’t just choose humans because they hate AI, but because the music made by AI just wasn’t that good compared to music made by humans. This also suggests that there is a creative difference between humans and AI. AI might be smarter in the way that they have information from all over the web, but that doesn’t mean that they are creative, because they are programmed to think in a certain way. Humans, on the other hand, are different; every single human on earth is unique and has their own way of thinking, so music made by humans will be creative because it is based on how the artist thinks.

AI and its complexities of ownership and copyright Using AI to compose music not only causes a change for the listeners, it also causes new problems for the music industry as a whole. The biggest problem is who is going to take ownership and copyright claims over the AI music. Drott presents a very good question on this topic “Who (or what) should be awarded rights over the products of so-called ‘expressive AI’: Its programmers? Its users? Or the AI itself? Largely overlooked in such debates is the status of another repertoire: not the music put out by an AI, but that which is put into it, the music that constitutes the training set necessary for machine learners to learn” (2020). There are many factors or people who can claim that they technically were responsible for the creation of the music created by AI. Programmers can say that they were responsible for creating the program to run the AI, Users can claim that they were the ones to input the prompts to get the AI to create the music, the AI is the one that created the music, but it has no way of claiming ownership since it’s not capable of doing so. You also have to look at what music the programmers use to train the AI to be able to create new music, so many factors can affect who has ownership and copyright claims. Additionally, what gives someone the right to copyright something that 1. It wasn’t made directly by them since it was made by AI, and 2. They only contributed to one part of the creation of the music. Surbhi states that “While existing legal frameworks offer some guidance, the complexities inherent in generative AI systems necessitate a balance between technological progress and legal remedies” (2024). Surbhi brings up a great point when he says that our current legal framework can be used as a form of guidance, AI is still something completely new that brings in completely new problems that our current legal framework doesn’t take into account. So, we have to use the already existing copyright framework and make changes to it to adapt to the technological progress of AI and its abilities. Surbhi concludes the article with “emphasizing the necessity of a paradigm shift in our understanding of creativity and a corresponding reassessment of our approach to copyright in the face of this technological dilemma” (2024). Surbhi gives a way to work around all the problems that come with the ownership of AI music. He begins by stating that we should change the way we think about creativity, which means that we should now consider how AI plays a role in the creative process of composing music. We shouldn’t let AI create all the music, but musicians can use AI to further improve their creativity and the quality of their music. This should naturally cause a change in the copyright laws, and now if AI were used to help a musician create the music, then not just the musician would be receiving credit, but also the people who trained/programmed the AI.

Largely overlooked in such debates is the status of another repertoire: not the music put out by an AI, but that which is put into it, the music that constitutes the training set necessary for machine learners to learn”
annotations.comment: I would suggest using a block quote to format citations over 40 words. Please also reduce the use of direct quotes and paraphrase the information in your own words.

paragraphs.body: AI as a tool to help musicians Musicians should not let AI take their place in composing their own music, but they should use AI as a tool to improve their music. This combines the creativity and real emotions of the musician with the boundless amount of knowledge that AI has; when combined, the musicians might see even more success, as their fans would be receiving higher-quality music. Additionally, using AI as a tool can be more efficient, as musicians can use AI to help them compose their music faster and more efficiently. A study on electrical dance music (EDM) conducted by Bohm shows that “the use of AI in production increased efficiency especially in the composition phase and the second editing phase” (2023). Electrical dance music is a genre of music that is usually used in parties so that people can dance to it, and Bohm’s study shows us that the use of AI made the production efficient in both speed and quality, especially in the composition and editing parts where AI’s processing speed can shine and save musicians time and energy. Bohm continues on to say that “There were no significant differences in the overall production ratio in the preparation phase, but this phase was slightly faster with human production” (2023). The preparation phase is the one before composing and editing, it is the phase where the musician starts to brainstorm ideas and the desired end goal of the music. It makes sense for this to be faster with humans since it is where their creativity and skills shine. This is also the phase where the emotions and personality/soul of the musician are put into the music, so not only is it faster with humans, based on what we said before, the musician will be better in this phase than AI because the musician will put emotions and soul into the music while the AI can’t. Bohm ends of the article by stating that “it should be noted that the introduction of AI as a tool into the right phase of the Production process has many positive aspects such as saving time and effort and supporting the creative process of composing melodies”, which is exactly what my claim is, AI should not be dependent on to compose music, it should only be a tool to help out the musician. In conclusion, even though AI can fully compose music, it shouldn’t be used to do that. Music that was composed by AI is seen negatively by both listeners and experts in the music industry, and this negative view is warranted as AI’s music is soulless and emotionless compared to music made by humans. On top of that, with the introduction of AI music, new problems with copyright and ownership have appeared. Nobody knows who should be credible, and many people contribute to AI music. Surbhi presented a good solution by using our existing legal framework as guidance to develop new ones containing solutions to the new problems of AI. Furthermore, musicians should give credit to programmers if a musician uses AI to help develop their music. The solution to AI music is that musicians should use AI to help develop and improve their music, not to take their role as musicians. This is the ideal outcome since it would combine the emotions and creativity of humans with the efficiency and knowledge of AI.
